steps.title: Run Built-In Diagnostics (If Available)
steps.instructions: Access system diagnostics or self-test functions. Expected: No probe or system errors reported. Why it matters: Confirms whether system detects a hardware fault.

CCR.Complaint: Ultrasound image shows excessive noise and poor resolution during cardiac exam.
CCR.Cause: Faulty phased array probe causing signal degradation and artifact.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced probe with known-good unit; image quality restored and system returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Presence of noise, lines, or dropout

title: Philips CX50 Ultrasound System - System Fails to Boot or Software Errors
description: Troubleshooting boot failures, freezing, or software errors on the Philips CX50 ultrasound system.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the system from clinical use before troubleshooting. If in use, transfer the patient to another imaging system.

steps.title: Verify Power Source and Basic Operation
steps.instructions: Check that the system is plugged into a known working outlet. Inspect the power cord and ensure it is fully seated. Attempt to power on using the main power button.

steps.title: Check Battery Status (Portable Operation)
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power and attempt to power on using battery. Reconnect AC and verify charging indicators.

steps.title: Perform a Full Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Shut down the system completely if partially responsive. Disconnect AC power and remove battery (if accessible). Wait 30–60 seconds, then reconnect and power on.

steps.title: Disconnect External Devices
steps.instructions: Remove all probes, USB devices, network cables, and peripherals. Attempt to boot with only essential components connected.

steps.title: Inspect Probes and Ports
steps.instructions: Check probe connectors for damage, bent pins, or debris. Leave probes disconnected during boot attempt.

steps.title: Observe Startup Behavior and Error Messages
steps.instructions: Note any error codes, beeps, or freeze points during boot. Document whether the system freezes at logo screen, displays software error messages, or reboots repeatedly.

steps.title: Check Cooling and Ventilation
steps.instructions: Ensure vents are not blocked and fans are operating. Listen for abnormal fan noise or lack of airflow.

steps.title: Attempt Alternate Power Source
steps.instructions: Plug the system into a different outlet or circuit.

steps.title: Restart and Attempt Multiple Boots
steps.instructions: Power cycle the system again and attempt multiple startups.

CCR.Complaint: Ultrasound system not booting, stuck on startup screen.
CCR.Cause: System failed to boot even after power cycle and removal of all peripherals, indicating likely internal hardware or software failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent to vendor for further evaluation and repair.

title: Philips CX50 Ultrasound System - Touchscreen or Keypad Not Responding
description: Troubleshooting unresponsive touchscreen or keypad inputs, delayed response, or inability to control system functions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the system from active patient use if controls are unresponsive. If in use, safely transition to another imaging system.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to use both the touchscreen and physical keypad. Observe for no response, partial response, or delayed/erratic behavior.

steps.title: Check for Screen Contamination or Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect for gel, fluid, debris, cracks, or damage. Clean the screen using approved wipes.

steps.title: Perform a System Reboot
steps.instructions: Power down completely, wait 30–60 seconds, then restart the system.

steps.title: Check for External Input Devices
steps.instructions: Disconnect any USB devices such as keyboard, mouse, or storage drives.

steps.title: Verify System is Not Frozen or Processing
steps.instructions: Check for freezing or lag and attempt to access menus or switch modes.

steps.title: Inspect Keypad for Physical Issues
steps.instructions: Check for stuck or damaged buttons and confirm consistent tactile response.

steps.title: Check Touchscreen Calibration (If Accessible)
steps.instructions: Navigate to calibration settings and attempt recalibration if available.

steps.title: Test in Different Modes or Applications
steps.instructions: Switch between imaging modes or menus to determine if issue is consistent or isolated.

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen and keypad not responding during imaging on Philips CX50.
CCR.Cause: Touchscreen remained unresponsive after cleaning and reboot, indicating likely internal hardware failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service and sent for repair evaluation.

title: Philips Brilliance Series CT Scanner - Gantry Rotation or Tilt Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for gantry rotation or tilt failure caused by obstruction, patient table interference, positioning limits, emergency stop, or internal motion control faults.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop the scan immediately if gantry motion is abnormal, noisy, stalled, or unpredictable. Action: Confirm the patient is not in contact with the gantry, table, or accessories. Move the patient out of the bore if safe to do so. Use another CT scanner or imaging method if the exam is urgent. Do not repeatedly attempt gantry movement with a patient positioned inside the bore. Expected: Patient is safely removed from any risk of collision, entrapment, or unexpected motion.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue involves gantry rotation, gantry tilt, or both. Action: Ask staff what motion failed. Note whether the issue occurred during setup, scout/topogram, helical acquisition, tilt command, or return-to-home movement. Check the operator console for error messages or motion inhibit warnings. Document whether motion is completely disabled, intermittent, noisy, or limited to one direction. Expected: The failure mode is clearly identified before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: 3. Check for Physical Obstruction Around the Gantry
steps.instructions: External obstruction is one of the first items to rule out. Action: Inspect the gantry opening, front cover, rear area, and floor space. Look for step stools, carts, positioning aids, linens, cables, IV poles, patient belongings, or contrast tubing near moving areas. Confirm nothing is caught between the gantry, table, or side covers. Expected: No object is blocking gantry rotation, tilt movement, or table clearance. If an obstruction is found and removed, retest motion only when the area is clear. If gantry movement returns normally, document the obstruction and return the scanner to service after functional verification.

steps.title: 4. Confirm Patient Table Position and Clearance
steps.instructions: Table position can prevent safe gantry motion or tilt. Action: Move the table out of the gantry if possible. Check that the tabletop is centered and not contacting the gantry. Confirm accessories such as head holders, body straps, arm boards, cushions, or immobilizers are not interfering. Verify the table height and horizontal position allow gantry tilt or rotation clearance. Expected: Table and accessories are clear of all gantry movement paths. If table repositioning resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the table/accessory interference.

steps.title: 5. Check Emergency Stop and Motion Inhibit Conditions
steps.instructions: Emergency stop or safety interlock conditions can disable gantry motion. Action: Inspect scanner emergency stop buttons at the gantry and operator area. Confirm no emergency stop is pressed or latched. Reset only according to site procedure. Check for room door, interlock, collision, or safety inhibit messages on the console. Expected: Emergency stop and safety inhibit conditions are cleared before motion is attempted. If an emergency stop was activated, verify why it was pressed before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: 6. Power Cycle the Operator Console and Gantry System if Safe
steps.instructions: Temporary software or control communication faults may prevent gantry movement. Action: Confirm no patient is on the table. Follow approved site shutdown and restart procedure. Allow the system to complete startup checks. Observe for gantry, tilt, or motor-related errors during initialization. Expected: The system restarts normally and gantry motion is restored if the issue was temporary. If the same error returns after restart, continue troubleshooting and do not keep cycling power repeatedly.

steps.title: 7. Test Gantry Motion Without a Patient
steps.instructions: Motion testing should be done only with the gantry area clear. Action: Confirm the bore, table, and floor area are clear. Attempt limited gantry tilt or rotation movement using normal controls. Listen for abnormal grinding, scraping, clicking, belt noise, motor strain, or sudden stopping. Watch for uneven motion, delayed response, or failure to reach commanded position. Expected: Gantry moves smoothly, quietly, and reaches the commanded position without error. If abnormal noise, jerky motion, or repeated motion failure occurs, stop testing and remove the scanner from service.

steps.title: 8. Check for Positioning Limit or Home Position Errors
steps.instructions: A gantry may fail motion commands if it cannot confirm its position. Action: Check the console for position, tilt angle, home position, encoder, limit, or calibration-related messages. Note whether the displayed gantry angle matches the physical position. Attempt only approved user-level return-to-neutral or home positioning if available. Expected: Gantry position feedback appears accurate and motion commands are accepted. If the system cannot confirm gantry position, escalation is required.

steps.title: 9. Inspect External Covers and Gantry Housing
steps.instructions: Loose or shifted covers can create rubbing, collision detection, or motion restriction. Action: Visually inspect the gantry covers for cracks, looseness, misalignment, or signs of impact. Look for scrape marks or areas where the gantry may be contacting trim or covers. Do not remove covers for internal inspection unless trained and authorized. Expected: Gantry covers are secure, aligned, and not interfering with motion. If cover damage or misalignment is found, remove the scanner from service until corrected.

steps.title: 10. Check Room Environment and Recent Events
steps.instructions: Environmental or operational events can help identify the cause. Action: Ask whether the scanner was recently serviced, moved, cleaned, bumped, or power cycled. Check for recent facility power issues. Verify room temperature is within normal operating expectations. Listen for cooling or system fans operating normally. Expected: No recent event explains a motion control fault. If the issue began after service, power loss, or impact, include that information in the work order.

steps.title: 11. Review Error Logs or Console Messages
steps.instructions: Clinical Engineering should capture useful fault information before escalation. Action: Record exact error messages, codes, timestamps, and symptoms. Note whether the fault occurred during gantry rotation, tilt, startup, or scan acquisition. Take photos of console messages if allowed by site policy. Avoid clearing logs before service review. Expected: Accurate fault details are preserved for repair or vendor support.

steps.title: 12. Determine Whether the Scanner Can Safely Return to Service
steps.instructions: Do not return the CT scanner to clinical use unless motion is normal and no faults remain. Action: Confirm gantry rotation and/or tilt function correctly. Verify no abnormal noise is present. Confirm no warning or inhibit messages remain. Confirm no patient safety risk exists. Expected: Scanner operates normally and safely. If any motion fault remains, keep the unit out of service.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported the Philips Brilliance CT scanner would not tilt the gantry and displayed a gantry motion failure during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no patient obstruction, no table interference, and no emergency stop condition, but gantry tilt failed again during no-patient motion testing.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, error details were documented, and the issue was escalated for Philips/vendor service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact console error message or code
Whether failure involved rotation, tilt, or both
Whether the issue occurred during startup, setup, scout, scan, or return-to-home movement
Patient table position at time of failure
Any abnormal noise, vibration, scraping, or motor strain
Whether gantry angle display matched physical position
Whether power cycle changed the condition
Recent service, cleaning, impact, or power event

title: Philips Brilliance Series CT Scanner - Tube Warm-Up or Exposure Errors
description: Tube warm-up, exposure inhibit, or scan start errors caused by readiness, power, cooling, or system interlock issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is actively being scanned or positioned for an exam before troubleshooting. Action: Stop the scan workflow. Move the patient off the table if the issue delays imaging. Notify CT staff that the scanner is not ready for patient use. Expected Outcome: Patient care is safely continued using another CT scanner or alternate imaging plan if needed. If this resolves the immediate clinical risk, continue troubleshooting only when the scanner is safe to evaluate.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Scan Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact message or behavior reported by CT staff. Action: Ask whether the issue occurred during tube warm-up, scout scan, helical scan, axial scan, or exposure attempt. Note the displayed message, exposure inhibit, tube not ready status, or aborted scan. Check whether the gantry, console, or operator interface shows additional warnings. Expected Outcome: The issue is confirmed and tied to tube readiness or exposure generation, not a general workflow issue.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Warm-Up Procedure Was Attempted
steps.instructions: CT tubes often require warm-up after idle time, startup, or room restart. Action: Ask CT staff whether the normal tube warm-up sequence was started. Confirm the scanner was not taken directly into patient scanning after extended idle time. Restart the warm-up only if the system allows it and no patient is on the table. Expected Outcome: Warm-up completes successfully and the scanner returns to ready status. If the warm-up completes and exposure is restored, document and return the unit to service.

steps.title: Check for Tube Overheat or Cooling Delay Messages
steps.instructions: Exposure may be inhibited if the tube is too hot or cooling calculations have not cleared. Action: Review console messages for tube heat, cooling delay, exposure inhibit, or tube unavailable status. Ask whether multiple high-dose or repeated scans were performed before the error. Allow the system to cool if the console indicates a cooling delay. Expected Outcome: Scanner clears the inhibit after the tube cooling period. If cooling clears the issue, verify normal scan readiness before returning to service.

steps.title: Verify Gantry and Room Power Status
steps.instructions: Unstable power or incomplete system startup can prevent exposure readiness. Action: Confirm the CT scanner, console, gantry, and associated equipment are powered normally. Check for tripped room emergency power-off conditions or facility power events. Verify no breaker, wall disconnect, or room power control was recently operated by Facilities. Expected Outcome: Scanner power is stable and the system is fully initialized. If a facility power issue is found, coordinate with Facilities before returning the scanner to service.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Interlock Conditions
steps.instructions: Exposure may be blocked if an emergency stop, door interlock, or safety circuit is active. Action: Verify all emergency stop buttons are released. Confirm room doors are fully closed if door interlocks are installed. Check for warning indicators at the console, gantry, or control area. Ask whether staff recently pressed an emergency stop or opened the room door during scanning. Expected Outcome: No active safety interlock is preventing exposure. If resetting an external safety condition clears the issue, perform a normal readiness check before clinical use.

steps.title: Inspect Console and Operator Workflow
steps.instructions: Some exposure errors are caused by incomplete protocol selection, scan prescription, or system state. Action: Confirm the exam is properly selected and the scanner is not waiting for a required user action. Verify the system is not paused, held, or in service mode. Have CT staff exit the scan setup and reselect the exam if safe to do so. Expected Outcome: Scanner accepts the scan workflow and allows exposure when ready. If the issue was workflow-related, document the finding and return the scanner to service.

steps.title: Restart the CT Application if Allowed by Site Procedure
steps.instructions: A software state issue can sometimes prevent tube warm-up or exposure readiness. Action: Confirm no patient is loaded for scanning. Have authorized CT staff close and reopen the scan application if permitted. If needed, perform a controlled console restart according to site procedure. Expected Outcome: System reloads normally and tube readiness can be rechecked. If the error clears after restart, monitor for recurrence and document the restart.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Reboot if Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the system remains inhibited and no active patient care is affected, a full controlled reboot may clear communication or readiness faults. Action: Notify CT leadership before rebooting. Follow site-approved shutdown and startup procedure. Allow the system to fully initialize. Attempt tube warm-up again if prompted by the system. Expected Outcome: Scanner initializes normally and completes warm-up without exposure errors. If the error returns after reboot, suspect a deeper system or tube/generator-related issue.

steps.title: Check Environmental and Cooling Conditions
steps.instructions: Tube and generator errors can occur if the room environment or cooling support is abnormal. Action: Check for excessive room temperature. Listen for abnormal cooling sounds from the gantry or equipment room. Look for water leaks, coolant alarms, unusual heat, or burning smell. Ask Facilities if HVAC or chilled water service recently changed. Expected Outcome: No environmental or cooling issue is found. If heat, odor, leakage, or cooling failure is present, remove the scanner from service and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Review Error Logs if Available to Clinical Engineering
steps.instructions: Clinical Engineering may be able to confirm whether the error is recurring or escalating. Action: Review accessible system event logs or error history. Note repeated tube warm-up failures, exposure inhibits, generator errors, rotor errors, or cooling-related messages. Avoid clearing logs before recording useful details. Expected Outcome: Error trend is documented for service escalation. If repeated tube, generator, rotor, or cooling faults are present, stop troubleshooting and escalate.

steps.title: Do Not Attempt Repeated Exposures
steps.instructions: Repeated exposure attempts can worsen tube stress or create unnecessary radiation-related risk. Action: Do not repeatedly force scan attempts. Do not bypass exposure inhibits, interlocks, or safety warnings. Stop after reasonable external checks are complete. Expected Outcome: The scanner is protected from further damage and patient/staff safety is maintained.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported the Philips Brilliance scanner would not complete tube warm-up and displayed an exposure inhibit before scanning.
CCR.Cause: Verified the error at the console and ruled out active emergency stop, door interlock, workflow setup, room power, and cooling delay conditions.
CCR.Resolution: Performed a controlled restart and repeated tube warm-up; error returned, so the scanner was removed from service and escalated for Philips service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact warm-up or exposure error message
Whether the issue occurred during warm-up, scout, axial, or helical scan
Whether the tube had been idle for an extended period
Recent high-volume scanning or repeated exposures
Tube cooling delay or heat status
Console restart or full system reboot performed
Cooling sounds, leaks, unusual heat, or odor

title: Philips Brilliance Series CT Scanner - Image Artifacts or Reconstruction Issues
description: Image artifacts, poor reconstruction, or distorted CT images caused by setup, patient positioning, calibration, accessory, or system issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is stable and the scan is not being repeated unnecessarily. Stop additional scanning until the artifact or reconstruction concern is understood. If imaging is clinically urgent, move the patient to another CT scanner if available. Notify the radiologist or CT lead if image quality may affect diagnosis. Expected: Patient exposure is minimized and clinical care continues safely.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Image Problem
steps.instructions: Review the reported artifact or reconstruction issue with the technologist or radiologist. Identify whether the issue is streaking, rings, banding, motion blur, shading, missing anatomy, poor reformats, or reconstruction failure. Determine if the problem occurred on one patient, one protocol, one body region, or all scans. Confirm whether raw data, axial images, reformats, or PACS images are affected. Expected: The issue is narrowed to acquisition, reconstruction, display, or transfer. Why it matters: CT image problems can come from patient motion, positioning, metal, protocol selection, detector calibration, reconstruction software, or PACS transfer.

steps.title: Check for Patient Motion or External Causes
steps.instructions: Motion and external objects are common causes of poor CT image quality. Ask whether the patient moved, coughed, breathed, or could not follow instructions. Check for external metal, jewelry, leads, ECG wires, oxygen tubing, monitoring cables, or positioning aids in the scan field. Confirm immobilization aids were used when appropriate. Expected: Artifact is explained by motion or external objects if only one patient or one study was affected. If this resolves the issue, document the cause and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Confirm Patient Positioning and Scan Centering
steps.instructions: Incorrect positioning can cause shading, truncation, or reconstruction problems. Verify the patient was centered properly in the gantry. Confirm the anatomy of interest was fully within the scan field of view. Review scout/localizer images for positioning errors. Check that arms, shoulders, or positioning devices were not outside the intended field in a way that caused truncation artifact. Expected: Patient and anatomy are properly centered within the selected field of view. Why it matters: CT reconstruction depends on accurate geometry and complete projection data.

steps.title: Review Protocol and Reconstruction Selection
steps.instructions: Incorrect protocol or reconstruction settings can create images that appear abnormal even when the scanner is functioning normally. Confirm the correct exam protocol was selected. Check slice thickness, kernel/filter, reconstruction field of view, iterative reconstruction setting, and reformatted image settings. Compare the current protocol against a known-good protocol for the same exam type. Verify pediatric, low-dose, metal artifact reduction, or specialized protocols were not selected by mistake. Expected: Protocol and reconstruction settings match the intended clinical exam. If protocol selection caused the issue, correct the protocol use and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Recent Changes or Software Behavior
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue began after a configuration, network, or software change. Ask whether there was recent service, software update, protocol editing, network change, or PACS issue. Check whether reconstruction jobs are delayed, stuck, failed, or producing incomplete image series. Restart only the workstation or reconstruction console if permitted by site policy and if no active patient scan is in progress. Confirm normal operation after restart using an approved test or phantom workflow. Expected: Temporary software or reconstruction queue issues clear after safe restart or job management.

steps.title: Inspect Accessories and Table-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Objects in the imaging path can create artifacts. Inspect the CT table pad, positioning straps, head holder, arm boards, and immobilization devices. Remove damaged, wet, folded, contaminated, or incorrect accessories from the scan field. Check for contrast spills, foreign objects, or dense materials under the patient. Confirm approved CT-compatible accessories are being used. Expected: No accessory or table item is causing attenuation or streak artifact.

steps.title: Check Gantry, Bore, and Visible Obstructions
steps.instructions: Visible contamination or obstruction can affect image quality. Inspect the gantry opening for obvious foreign material, loose covers, damaged panels, or debris. Check for fluid spills, contrast leakage, tape, packaging, or patient items near the bore. Listen for abnormal gantry noise during a non-patient movement check if site policy allows. Do not remove covers or access internal gantry components. Expected: Gantry area is clean, intact, and free of visible obstruction.

steps.title: Perform Approved Quality Control or Phantom Check
steps.instructions: Use the site-approved CT QC process to determine whether the artifact is reproducible without a patient. Run the appropriate daily QC, air calibration, water phantom, or image quality check according to local procedure. Review whether the artifact appears on phantom images. Note any failed calibration, detector, tube, gantry, or reconstruction-related warnings. Compare results to prior QC records if available. Expected: QC passes and images are free of artifacts. If QC fails or the artifact appears on phantom images, remove the scanner from clinical use and escalate.

steps.title: Check Reconstruction Console and Storage Status
steps.instructions: Reconstruction issues may occur if the system cannot process or store image data correctly. Verify the reconstruction console is powered on and responsive. Check for full storage, frozen applications, failed reconstruction jobs, or excessive backlog. Confirm image series are being generated completely before transfer to PACS. Check whether the issue is local to the CT console or only appears after network/PACS transfer. Expected: Reconstruction completes normally and images are available locally before transfer. Why it matters: A PACS or network issue can look like a reconstruction issue if images are missing, delayed, or incomplete.

steps.title: Verify Network and PACS Transfer Separately
steps.instructions: If reconstructed images look correct locally but wrong or incomplete in PACS, troubleshoot the image transfer path. Confirm local CT images are complete and usable. Check whether all expected series were sent to PACS. Resend the affected series if appropriate. Verify network connectivity and DICOM destination status. Contact PACS or IT support if local images are normal but PACS images are missing, incomplete, or displayed incorrectly. Expected: The scanner reconstruction is confirmed separate from PACS transfer or display issues.

steps.title: Review System Messages and Error Logs at the Operator Level
steps.instructions: Check for visible system messages without performing internal service procedures. Review current system alerts, reconstruction errors, calibration messages, or detector-related warnings. Record exact error text, time, scan protocol, and affected exam. Avoid clearing service messages before documentation unless required for safe workflow. Do not perform service-level calibration or internal diagnostics unless authorized. Expected: Any system fault information is captured for service evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported streak artifacts and incomplete reconstructed image series on a Philips Brilliance CT scan.
CCR.Cause: Patient motion and external objects were ruled out, protocol settings were verified, and phantom QC reproduced image artifacts.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to Philips service for detector/reconstruction system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether one patient or multiple patients were affected
Whether artifact appears on scout, axial, reformatted, or PACS images
Power behavior or restart behavior of the reconstruction console
Environmental factors such as temperature, humidity, recent room work, or contrast spill
Indicator lights or visible system alerts
Exact system messages or error codes

title: Philips Brilliance Series CT Scanner - Table Motion or Positioning Errors
description: Table movement, indexing, positioning, or height errors caused by obstructions, controls, accessories, power, or positioning system faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not continue scanning if the table is moving unpredictably, not stopping correctly, or positioning inaccurately. Stop the scan or planned scan. Keep the patient still and supported. Remove the patient from the scanner table if movement is unsafe or unreliable. Use another CT room if the exam is urgent. Expected outcome: Patient safety is maintained before troubleshooting begins. If this resolves the immediate safety concern, stop and document the issue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Table Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact behavior before assuming a table failure. Ask staff what occurred. Determine if the issue is: table will not move, table moves intermittently, table height will not adjust, table indexing is inaccurate, table position does not match console display, table stops during movement, or table produces abnormal noise. Note whether the issue occurs from the gantry controls, table controls, or operator console. Expected outcome: The table motion or positioning problem is clearly identified and reproducible.

steps.title: Confirm the Table Is Clear of the Patient and Staff
steps.instructions: Physical obstruction is one of the first things to rule out. Ensure the patient, staff, blankets, straps, IV lines, tubing, or accessories are not restricting table travel. Check that nothing is caught between the tabletop, base, gantry opening, or floor area. Verify the table is not contacting another object in the room. Expected outcome: Table travel path is clear. If the table moves normally after clearing the obstruction, return the unit to service after verifying safe operation and documenting the finding.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Motion Inhibit Conditions
steps.instructions: A CT table may be prevented from moving if an emergency stop or safety interlock is active. Check gantry and console emergency stop buttons. Confirm none are pressed or latched. Reset only according to site procedure. Look for system messages indicating motion inhibit, collision prevention, or safety stop. Expected outcome: No emergency stop or safety inhibit is active. If resetting a known activated emergency stop restores table motion, verify full table operation before returning to service.

steps.title: Inspect Table Controls and Gantry Control Panels
steps.instructions: A stuck or damaged control can cause no movement, intermittent movement, or unexpected behavior. Inspect table-side controls for stuck buttons, damage, fluid intrusion, or loose panels. Test table motion from available control locations if safe. Compare behavior between the operator console and gantry/table controls. Expected outcome: Controls respond consistently and do not appear stuck, damaged, or contaminated. If one control location fails but another works normally, document the failed control location and remove the scanner from service if safe operation cannot be guaranteed.

steps.title: Check Table Position Limits
steps.instructions: The table may stop moving if it has reached a mechanical or programmed travel limit. Verify whether the table is already at its upper, lower, in, out, or longitudinal travel limit. Attempt movement in the opposite direction if safe. Observe whether the system displays a limit, positioning, or travel warning. Expected outcome: The table either moves away from the limit or confirms a travel-limit condition. If the table was simply at a travel limit and normal movement resumes, verify full operation and document the finding.

steps.title: Verify Patient Weight and Table Load
steps.instructions: Excessive load or uneven loading can affect table lift and positioning accuracy. Confirm patient weight is within the scanner table’s rated capacity. Check for excessive accessory weight on the table. Ensure the patient is centered and not causing uneven table loading. Remove unnecessary accessories before retesting. Expected outcome: The table is not overloaded or unevenly loaded. If table motion returns after correcting load conditions, document the cause and remind staff to follow table weight and positioning limits.

steps.title: Inspect Tabletop, Cradle, and Accessories
steps.instructions: Misaligned or loose accessories can interfere with table motion or indexing. Inspect the tabletop/cradle for visible damage, loose hardware, cracked covers, or abnormal gaps. Verify head holders, pads, straps, positioning aids, and extension accessories are correctly installed. Remove nonessential accessories and retest table movement if safe. Expected outcome: No accessory or tabletop issue is interfering with table travel. If an accessory was causing the issue, remove it from use and document the correction.

steps.title: Check for Abnormal Noise, Binding, or Vibration
steps.instructions: Mechanical resistance can indicate a table drive, rail, bearing, lift, or alignment issue. Move the table slowly through a limited safe range with no patient present. Listen for grinding, clicking, scraping, slipping, or sudden stops. Watch for jerky movement or uneven lift. Stop testing immediately if motion appears unsafe. Expected outcome: Table motion is smooth and controlled. If abnormal noise, binding, or vibration is present, remove the scanner from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Verify Console Position Display and Table Position Accuracy
steps.instructions: A positioning mismatch can affect scan planning and image location accuracy. Compare displayed table position with observed physical table position. Check whether table coordinates update smoothly during motion. Attempt a controlled positioning movement only with no patient present. Confirm whether the system reports positioning, encoder, or table feedback errors. Expected outcome: Displayed table position matches physical table movement. If the table moves but position feedback is inaccurate, remove the scanner from service. Incorrect table position can directly affect exam accuracy.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Restart if Safe
steps.instructions: Temporary software or communication faults can affect table control. Confirm no patient is on the table. Follow department downtime and restart procedure. Power down or restart the CT system according to site policy. Allow the system to fully initialize. Retest limited table movement without a patient. Expected outcome: Table motion and position display return to normal if the issue was temporary. If the issue clears after restart, perform a safe operational check and document the restart and successful table test.

steps.title: Check for Room Power or System Power Issues
steps.instructions: Table drive and control systems require stable power. Verify the CT system is fully powered and not in a partial shutdown state. Check for facility power events, breaker trips, UPS alarms, or recent room power interruptions. Confirm the operator console and gantry are communicating normally. Do not access internal power components unless trained and authorized. Expected outcome: No external facility power issue is contributing to the table fault. If power instability is suspected, involve facilities and vendor service as appropriate.

steps.title: Review Error Messages and Event History
steps.instructions: Table errors may be linked to position sensors, drive faults, collision detection, or communication issues. Record the exact error message or code shown on the console. Note when the error occurs: startup, table movement, scan setup, or during exam. Capture screenshots or photos if allowed by facility policy. Include gantry/table position and patient load status at time of failure. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is documented clearly for repair escalation.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported the Philips Brilliance CT table would not advance into the gantry and displayed a table positioning error during exam setup.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no patient obstruction, no activated emergency stop, and normal accessory setup, but table motion remained intermittent with inaccurate position feedback.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, error details were documented, and Philips service was contacted for table drive and position feedback evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact table error message or code
Whether issue occurred with or without patient loaded
Direction of failed motion: up, down, in, out, longitudinal
Whether gantry controls and console controls behave differently

title: Philips Brilliance Series CT Scanner - System Boot or Software Failure
description: CT scanner startup, console boot, application launch, or software loading failures caused by power, network, peripheral, or system issues.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is on the table or actively being scanned before troubleshooting. Action: Stop any attempted exam. Remove the patient from the scanner room if clinically appropriate. Notify CT staff that the scanner is not available for use. Use another scanner if imaging is urgent. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely without relying on an unstable CT system.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Boot or Software Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm what part of the system is failing. Action: Ask CT staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue affects the operator console, gantry, acquisition system, reconstruction system, or all components. Note any displayed error messages, frozen screens, failed application launches, or startup loops. Check whether the failure occurs during power-up, login, exam launch, scanning, or image reconstruction. Expected outcome: The failure point is clearly identified before additional troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: 3. Check for Active Patient or Exam Data Concerns
steps.instructions: Before restarting or power cycling, confirm whether any patient exam data may be unsaved or mid-transfer. Action: Ask CT staff if an exam was in progress. Check whether images were acquired but not sent to PACS. Confirm whether the system is frozen during reconstruction, transfer, or exam closure. Do not force shutdown if data recovery may still be possible unless patient or equipment safety requires it. Expected outcome: Potential patient data loss is considered before rebooting.

steps.title: 4. Check Main System Power
steps.instructions: Boot failures may be caused by incomplete or unstable power to the CT system. Action: Verify the CT system, console, monitors, gantry, and computer cabinets are powered. Check for tripped breakers, emergency power-off activation, UPS alarms, or power distribution faults. Confirm the room has normal facility power. Look for abnormal power indicators on console equipment or system cabinets. Expected outcome: External power problems are identified or ruled out. If restoring proper power resolves the issue, confirm normal boot and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 5. Check the Emergency Stop and System Interlock Status
steps.instructions: A pressed emergency stop or active interlock may prevent full system startup. Action: Inspect all accessible emergency stop buttons. Confirm none are pressed or latched. Check scanner room door/interlock status if applicable. Ask CT staff whether an emergency stop was used recently. Expected outcome: The scanner is not being held in an inhibited state by an external safety control. If resetting an active emergency stop allows the system to boot normally, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: 6. Inspect Console Workstation Power and Peripherals
steps.instructions: A console issue may appear as a CT system software failure. Action: Verify the workstation is powered on. Check monitor power, video cables, keyboard, mouse, and network connections. Confirm all required console monitors are displaying properly. Look for frozen screens, blank displays, or failed operating system startup. Expected outcome: A simple workstation, display, or peripheral issue is ruled out before assuming CT software failure.

steps.title: 7. Check Network and Data Connections
steps.instructions: Some CT systems may fail to fully launch applications or complete startup if required network paths are unavailable. Action: Inspect visible network cables at the console and equipment cabinet. Confirm cables are fully seated and not damaged. Check for disconnected wall data ports or patch cables. Ask staff whether PACS, worklist, or hospital network issues are occurring elsewhere. Expected outcome: Network-related startup or application loading issues are considered. If reconnecting a loose network cable resolves the startup issue, verify normal operation and stop.

steps.title: 8. Perform a Controlled Software Restart
steps.instructions: If the console is responsive enough, use the normal shutdown or restart process. Action: Close the CT application using the standard system workflow if possible. Restart the application or workstation through normal software controls. Avoid unplugging equipment or forcing shutdown unless the system is completely unresponsive. Observe whether the CT software launches normally after restart. Expected outcome: Temporary software lockups clear without unnecessary hard power cycling. If the system boots and software loads normally, perform basic operational checks and stop.

steps.title: 9. Perform a Full Controlled System Reboot
steps.instructions: If a software restart does not resolve the issue, perform a controlled system reboot according to site policy. Action: Notify CT staff before rebooting. Follow the normal Philips system shutdown and startup sequence used at the facility. Allow the system enough time to fully power down and restart. Observe startup messages, application launch, gantry communication, and reconstruction system status. Expected outcome: Temporary communication or software initialization faults may clear after a complete controlled reboot. If the system returns to normal operation, document the reboot and final status.

steps.title: 10. Check for Repeated Boot Loops or Recurring Software Errors
steps.instructions: Recurring failure after restart suggests a deeper system issue. Action: Observe whether the same failure repeats. Note exact error messages, codes, timestamps, and startup stage. Record whether the console OS loads but the CT application fails, or whether the workstation itself fails to boot. Check for unusual sounds, cabinet alarms, overheating, or warning indicators. Expected outcome: Escalation information is captured accurately for repair support.

steps.title: 11. Check Environmental Conditions Around System Electronics
steps.instructions: Overheating or poor airflow can cause software instability or failed startup. Action: Inspect console and equipment cabinet ventilation. Confirm air vents are not blocked. Check for excessive heat around the console, reconstruction hardware, or equipment cabinets. Listen for abnormal fan noise or missing fan operation. Report room HVAC problems if the CT equipment room is unusually warm. Expected outcome: External environmental causes are ruled out before assuming internal computer or software failure.

steps.title: 12. Confirm System Status After Recovery
steps.instructions: If the system boots successfully, verify basic readiness before returning it to service. Action: Confirm the CT application opens normally. Verify gantry and table communication. Confirm no active system errors remain. Confirm worklist, image reconstruction, and image send functions are available if appropriate. Ask CT staff to perform normal startup checks or required QA before clinical use. Expected outcome: The scanner is not returned to service based only on a successful boot screen.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported the Philips Brilliance CT scanner would not complete startup and the CT application failed to launch.
CCR.Cause: Verified console power was present, network connections were seated, emergency stop was not active, and the system continued to freeze during software startup after controlled reboot.
CCR.Resolution: Removed scanner from service, labeled Out of Service, documented recurring startup failure, and escalated to imaging service/Philips support for further evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or exact error message
Whether the console, gantry, or full system failed to boot
Exact error message or startup screen where failure occurred
Whether patient data or image transfer was in progress
Whether facility power and UPS status were normal
Whether emergency stop or door interlock was active
Whether network cables were checked or reseated
Whether the system froze, rebooted, or stayed blank
Accessories swapped or console peripherals checked
Power behavior and indicator lights on console, gantry, cabinets, or UPS
Environmental factors such as unusual fan noise, heat, odor, or cabinet alarms
Whether controlled reboot changed the behavior
Final device status and escalation path

title: Philips Ingenuity Series CT Scanner - Gantry Rotation or Vibration Faults
description: Gantry rotation faults, vibration, abnormal noise, or scan interruption caused by external obstruction, setup issues, or mechanical rotation concerns.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Stop the scan if gantry vibration, abnormal noise, rotation fault, or unexpected gantry behavior occurs. Action: Remove the patient from the scanner table when safe to do so. Do not continue scanning until the fault is evaluated. Expected outcome: Patient is safely removed from the affected equipment and moved to an alternate scanner if imaging is urgent. Why it matters: Gantry rotation issues can affect patient safety, image quality, and equipment integrity.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact reported behavior before resetting or clearing anything. Action: Review the operator console message, fault code, scan phase, and whether the issue occurred during startup, positioning, scout, or active scan. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is documented clearly. Why it matters: A vibration during gantry acceleration may point in a different direction than a rotation fault at startup.

steps.title: 3. Check for External Obstruction Around the Gantry
steps.instructions: Inspect the gantry opening and surrounding area. Action: Look for linens, positioning straps, contrast tubing, ECG leads, patient belongings, table accessories, or foreign objects near the gantry bore or rotating path. Expected outcome: No item is contacting or interfering with the gantry area. If obstruction is found, remove it, verify the area is clear, and attempt system operation only if no damage, noise, or vibration remains. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 4. Inspect the Patient Table Position and Accessories
steps.instructions: Confirm the table is not mispositioned or causing contact during gantry movement. Action: Check table height, longitudinal position, head holder, arm boards, immobilization devices, and accessories. Expected outcome: The table and accessories are positioned correctly and are not contacting the gantry. Why it matters: Incorrect table or accessory setup can mimic a gantry mechanical fault.

steps.title: 5. Confirm Room and Gantry Area Are Clear
steps.instructions: Make sure no external equipment is interfering with scanner operation. Action: Verify IV poles, injector lines, oxygen tubing, portable equipment, cables, and cleaning supplies are clear of the gantry and table travel path. Expected outcome: The scanner area is clear and safe for movement. If interference is found and corrected, retest only if the scanner shows no abnormal noise or vibration.

steps.title: 6. Listen for Abnormal Mechanical Noise
steps.instructions: Assess the type and timing of the sound. Action: From a safe distance, note whether the sound is grinding, scraping, knocking, thumping, rhythmic vibration, or high-pitched bearing-like noise. Expected outcome: Noise is either absent after external checks or remains present and is documented. Why it matters: Persistent mechanical noise after external causes are ruled out suggests internal gantry drive, bearing, belt, brake, or rotating assembly concerns.

steps.title: 7. Check for Visible Vibration or Imbalance
steps.instructions: Observe the gantry during a safe non-patient test sequence only if allowed by site procedure. Action: Watch for shaking, uneven rotation, sudden stops, or vibration that increases with speed. Expected outcome: Gantry motion is smooth and stable. If vibration remains, stop testing and remove the scanner from service.

steps.title: 8. Review Console Messages and System Status
steps.instructions: Check for active or recurring gantry-related faults. Action: Review the operator console for rotation fault messages, gantry drive errors, tilt/position errors, emergency stop history, or scan abort messages. Expected outcome: Fault information is captured for service documentation. Why it matters: Recurring gantry faults usually require Philips service diagnostics rather than continued frontline troubleshooting.

steps.title: 9. Check Emergency Stop and Gantry Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: Verify that an emergency stop or safety circuit was not activated. Action: Inspect gantry, console, and room emergency stop buttons. Confirm none are engaged. Check door/interlock indicators if applicable. Expected outcome: Emergency stop and safety interlock status is normal. If an emergency stop was engaged, follow facility reset procedure and verify no unsafe condition caused the activation.

steps.title: 10. Power Cycle Only if Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If there is no abnormal noise, vibration, burning smell, visible damage, or active safety concern, perform a controlled restart per site procedure. Action: Coordinate with CT staff, stop scanning activity, and perform the approved shutdown/restart sequence. Expected outcome: Temporary software or drive initialization faults clear. If the gantry fault returns, do not continue repeated resets.

steps.title: 11. Do Not Continue Scanning With Persistent Gantry Vibration
steps.instructions: If abnormal vibration, scraping, grinding, repeated scan aborts, or gantry rotation faults continue, stop use. Action: Remove the scanner from clinical service and notify imaging leadership and service support. Expected outcome: The scanner is not used until evaluated. Why it matters: Continuing to operate a CT scanner with possible gantry rotation instability can worsen damage and create patient safety risk.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported a Philips Ingenuity Series CT scanner aborted during scan setup with gantry rotation fault and noticeable vibration from the gantry.
CCR.Cause: External obstruction, table accessories, room equipment, and emergency stop status were checked with no external cause found; vibration remained during safe system check.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, imaging leadership was notified, and Philips service was requested for gantry rotation system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Outlet or power source status if checked
Exact console fault message or error code
When the fault occurred: startup, scout, scan, or shutdown
Whether vibration was visible or only audible
Type of noise: grinding, scraping, knocking, thumping, or humming
Whether table position or accessories were involved
Whether the fault cleared after restart
Any unusual heat, smell, smoke, or visible damage
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Philips Ingenuity Series CT Scanner - Tube or Generator Errors
description: Tube or generator errors preventing CT exposure, scan start, tube warm-up, or normal image acquisition.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is being scanned or positioned for exposure while the tube or generator error is active. Action: Stop the exam if safe to do so. Remove the patient from the scanner room if additional troubleshooting is needed. Notify CT staff that the scanner should not be used until cleared. Expected outcome: Patient safety is maintained and no unintended exposure occurs. If patient care is urgent, move the patient to another CT scanner or alternate imaging pathway.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact tube or generator error message shown on the CT console. Action: Ask the technologist what occurred before the error. Note whether the error appeared during startup, tube warm-up, scout/topogram, axial scan, helical scan, or after repeated scans. Record any displayed error code or fault text. Check whether exposure was inhibited or aborted. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is clearly identified before power or environmental checks are performed. Why it matters: Tube and generator errors can be caused by overheating, power issues, failed warm-up, exposure inhibit conditions, or internal high-voltage faults.

steps.title: Check for Active Exposure Inhibit or System Not Ready Status
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the scanner is preventing exposure because the system is not ready. Action: Review the console status messages. Look for tube cooling, gantry, table, interlock, emergency stop, or system ready indicators. Confirm the system is not waiting for a required warm-up, calibration, or reset. Expected outcome: Scanner status clearly shows whether the exposure is inhibited by a correctable condition. If a simple readiness condition clears and scanning resumes normally, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Room Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: A tripped emergency stop or room interlock can prevent tube operation and mimic generator-related failure. Action: Confirm all emergency stop buttons are released. Check control room and scan room emergency stop locations. Verify scan room door status if door interlock is used. Confirm no safety interlock warning remains on the console. Expected outcome: Emergency stop and room interlock circuits are normal. If an emergency stop or interlock caused the issue, reset according to site procedure, verify normal system readiness, and stop.

steps.title: Verify Main Power and Facility Supply
steps.instructions: Tube and generator systems require stable facility power. Poor incoming power can cause exposure faults, generator errors, or failed tube warm-up. Action: Confirm the CT system is powered normally. Check for recent power interruptions, generator transfer events, storms, electrical work, or breaker trips. Verify the CT power panel and disconnect are in the correct operating position. Contact facilities if there are signs of unstable incoming power. Expected outcome: Facility power appears stable and appropriate for CT operation. Why it matters: CT generators draw high power during exposure. A marginal supply issue may only appear when the system attempts to scan.

steps.title: Check Console and System Communication Status
steps.instructions: Some tube or generator faults may be reported when the console cannot properly communicate with scanner subsystems. Action: Confirm the operator console is responsive. Check for system communication errors, subsystem offline messages, or startup failures. Verify no network or host-related issue is preventing normal scan control. Expected outcome: Console and scanner subsystems appear online and communicating normally. If the system is frozen, nonresponsive, or showing multiple subsystem errors, proceed with a controlled restart if permitted by site procedure.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Restart
steps.instructions: A temporary software or subsystem initialization issue may cause a generator or tube fault. Action: Confirm no patient is on the table. Follow the site-approved shutdown process. Allow the CT system to fully power down or complete the restart sequence. Restart the system and observe startup status. Do not repeatedly reset the system if the same error returns. Expected outcome: Temporary startup or communication faults clear after one controlled restart. If the error clears and tube warm-up/scanner readiness completes normally, return the system to service per department policy and document the action.

steps.title: Allow Tube Cooling Time if Overheating Is Suspected
steps.instructions: Tube heat load can trigger exposure inhibit, tube cooling, or generator-related errors. Action: Ask CT staff whether multiple high-dose or back-to-back exams were performed. Check console heat/cooling messages if available. Allow the tube to cool according to displayed system guidance. Avoid forcing repeated warm-up or exposure attempts. Expected outcome: Cooling-related messages clear after adequate cool-down time. Why it matters: Repeated exposure attempts during high tube heat can worsen tube stress and increase downtime. If the scanner returns to ready status after cooling, perform only normal clinical readiness checks required by local policy and stop.

steps.title: Verify Tube Warm-Up Status
steps.instructions: If the issue occurs during startup or before the first scan of the day, the tube may require warm-up. Action: Ask whether normal daily startup or tube warm-up was completed. Attempt only the manufacturer-approved warm-up process available to CT staff or service-trained personnel. Observe whether the warm-up starts, completes, aborts, or triggers the same error. Expected outcome: Warm-up completes without tube or generator error. If warm-up repeatedly fails, remove the scanner from service and escalate.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Cooling Issues
steps.instructions: Generator and tube operation depend on proper room temperature, airflow, and cooling support. Action: Check scan room and equipment room temperature. Look for blocked vents, restricted airflow, or equipment room HVAC problems. Listen for unusual cooling fan noise. Check for abnormal heat or smell near equipment cabinets without opening panels. Notify facilities if room cooling appears abnormal. Expected outcome: The CT room and equipment space are within normal operating conditions with no obvious cooling issue. Why it matters: Overtemperature conditions can cause tube, generator, or power electronics faults.

steps.title: Inspect External Cables and Visible Connections Only
steps.instructions: Do not open generator cabinets or access high-voltage areas during basic troubleshooting. Action: Visually inspect accessible external cables for damage, strain, or loose connections. Check console, gantry, and visible system cabling only where safely accessible. Do not reseat internal generator, tube, or high-voltage connections unless authorized and trained. Expected outcome: No visible external cable damage or unsafe condition is found. If damage, burning, arcing smell, fluid intrusion, or unusual heat is observed, stop troubleshooting and remove the system from service.

steps.title: Check for Recent Service, Tube Change, or Software Activity
steps.instructions: Recent maintenance can help identify whether the issue is related to configuration, calibration, or component replacement. Action: Ask CT staff whether recent service was performed. Check work order history for tube replacement, generator repair, software updates, calibrations, or recurring exposure faults. Note whether the current error matches a recent pattern. Expected outcome: Prior service history helps determine whether vendor or internal repair escalation is needed.

steps.title: Avoid Repeated Exposure Attempts
steps.instructions: Repeated attempts to scan through a tube or generator fault can create unnecessary risk. Action: Do not keep retrying exposures after repeated tube/generator errors. Do not bypass interlocks or readiness messages. Do not perform internal high-voltage troubleshooting during basic Clinical Engineering response. Expected outcome: The system is protected from additional stress and patient safety is maintained.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported the Philips Ingenuity CT scanner would not scan due to a tube/generator error during tube warm-up.
CCR.Cause: Verified repeated exposure inhibit after warm-up attempt; emergency stops, room interlock, facility power, and visible external conditions checked with no external cause found.
CCR.Resolution: Removed scanner from service, labeled Out of Service, documented error behavior, and escalated to Philips service for tube/generator evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact tube or generator error message
When the fault occurred: startup, warm-up, scout, scan, or post-scan
Whether exposure was inhibited or aborted
Facility power status or recent power events
Whether tube cooling message was present
Whether tube warm-up completed or failed
Unusual sounds, heat, odor, or visible damage

title: Philips Ingenuity Series CT Scanner - Detector Calibration or Image Artifacts
description: Detector calibration failures or image artifacts caused by setup, patient positioning, table, room, accessory, or calibration-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is not actively being scanned before troubleshooting. Action: Stop the scan if image quality is unsafe for diagnosis. Remove the patient from the scanner if repeated calibration or artifact issues occur. Notify clinical staff that the scanner should not be used for patient imaging until image quality is verified. Expected outcome: Patient safety is maintained and no unreliable images are used for diagnosis. If this resolves the immediate safety concern, continue troubleshooting with the scanner out of patient use.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Artifact or Calibration Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is being reported. Action: Ask whether the issue is a detector calibration failure, ring artifact, streak artifact, shading, banding, noisy image, or reconstruction-related artifact. Review the affected exam type, protocol, body part, and timing. Determine whether the issue occurs on every scan or only certain protocols. Expected outcome: The issue is clearly defined before parts or internal failures are suspected. Why it matters: Different artifact patterns can point toward patient motion, metal, calibration drift, detector issues, or reconstruction problems.

steps.title: Check for Immediate Scan Setup Issues
steps.instructions: Rule out common non-equipment causes first. Action: Confirm the patient was centered properly in the gantry. Check that positioning aids, straps, blankets, ECG leads, oxygen tubing, or accessories were not in the scan field. Ask whether the patient moved during acquisition. Check for large metal implants, external hardware, contrast injector tubing, or arm positioning problems. Expected outcome: Artifact is explained by positioning, motion, or external objects. If corrected positioning or removal of external objects resolves the image artifact, return the scanner to service after confirming acceptable test images.

steps.title: Inspect the Gantry Opening and Scan Field
steps.instructions: Look for anything physically interfering with image acquisition. Action: Inspect the bore, covers, table pad, head holder, positioning accessories, and scan field. Remove loose objects, debris, contrast tubing, ECG wires, or unused positioning devices. Verify that no accessory is protruding into the imaging field. Expected outcome: The scan field is clear and free of artifact-causing objects. Why it matters: Objects in or near the scan field can create streaking, shading, or unexpected artifact patterns.

steps.title: Verify Table Positioning and Movement
steps.instructions: Table alignment problems can mimic detector or reconstruction issues. Action: Move the table through its normal travel range without a patient. Listen for abnormal noise or hesitation. Check that the table moves smoothly and returns to expected positions. Confirm table height and centering are appropriate. Expected outcome: Table motion is smooth and positioning appears normal. If table movement is abnormal, remove the scanner from service and escalate for repair evaluation before continuing clinical use.

steps.title: Check Room and Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Environmental problems can affect calibration stability and image quality. Action: Confirm the CT room temperature is within the facility’s normal operating range. Check for unusual heat, humidity, water leaks, air conditioning failure, or airflow issues. Verify that gantry ventilation openings are not blocked. Look for signs of overheating, unusual smell, or warning messages. Expected outcome: The scanner environment is stable and appropriate for CT operation. Why it matters: Detector and electronics performance can be affected by temperature instability or poor ventilation.

steps.title: Confirm System Readiness and Warm-Up Status
steps.instructions: Detector calibration and image quality may be affected if the system has not completed normal readiness routines. Action: Confirm the scanner is fully booted and ready. Review system messages for warm-up, calibration, detector, reconstruction, or temperature warnings. Confirm any required daily startup or quality check process was completed by the imaging team according to site procedure. Expected outcome: The system is in a ready state with no active warnings preventing proper imaging. If the system indicates required warm-up or calibration is incomplete, allow the appropriate user-level process to complete before further testing.

steps.title: Run User-Level Image Quality or Phantom Check if Available
steps.instructions: Use site-approved QA checks to confirm whether the artifact is reproducible. Action: Use the facility’s approved CT QA phantom or image quality test process. Perform only approved user-level or Clinical Engineering-supported checks. Compare image appearance with expected baseline results. Note whether artifacts appear in the same location, same slice range, or same protocol. Expected outcome: Image quality is either confirmed acceptable or the artifact is reproduced under controlled conditions. Why it matters: A reproducible artifact on a phantom is stronger evidence of equipment-related failure than a single patient image.

steps.title: Check for Protocol-Specific or Reconstruction-Specific Patterns
steps.instructions: Determine whether the problem is linked to scanning or image processing. Action: Ask CT staff whether raw acquisition completed normally. Compare different protocols if safe and permitted by site procedure. Check whether artifacts appear only after reconstruction, only on certain kernels, or only on certain image series. Review whether the scanner console shows reconstruction delays, failed series, or processing errors. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to acquisition, detector calibration, reconstruction, or protocol behavior. If the issue appears only with certain protocols, escalate to CT applications support or service support as appropriate.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes or Service Activity
steps.instructions: Identify changes that may have triggered the issue. Action: Ask whether there was recent service, software update, calibration, power interruption, tube replacement, detector work, room temperature event, or construction near the scanner. Check whether the artifact began immediately after one of these events. Review recent work orders for related scanner issues. Expected outcome: A recent change is identified or ruled out. Why it matters: Detector calibration and image artifact problems often appear after service activity, environmental changes, or incomplete calibration recovery.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only if Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: A controlled restart may clear temporary software or reconstruction issues. Action: Confirm no patient is on the table. Confirm with CT staff that the scanner can be taken down. Perform a normal shutdown and restart according to facility procedure. Do not interrupt power during active shutdown or scanning. Expected outcome: Temporary software or reconstruction-related issue clears after restart. If the artifact or calibration failure returns after restart, continue escalation.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Scanner Can Safely Return to Service
steps.instructions: Do not return the scanner to clinical use based only on the absence of an error message. Action: Confirm test imaging or QA results are acceptable. Confirm no active detector, calibration, reconstruction, gantry, or system warnings remain. Confirm CT staff agree image quality appears clinically acceptable. Document the checks performed and final status. Expected outcome: Scanner is either verified for use or removed from service. If artifact remains, calibration fails, or image quality cannot be verified, stop troubleshooting and escalate.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported repeated detector calibration failure and ring-like image artifacts on the Philips Ingenuity CT scanner during routine scanning.
CCR.Cause: External scan field, table movement, patient positioning, room conditions, and accessories were checked; artifact remained during controlled image quality testing.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to Philips service for detector calibration and image acquisition evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether artifact occurred on patient images, phantom images, or both
Artifact type: ring, streak, shading, banding, noise, missing data, or reconstruction failure
Whether artifact appears in the same image location
Whether detector calibration passed or failed
Any gantry, detector, reconstruction, or temperature messages
Recent service, software updates, or power events
Accessories or objects found in scan field
Power behavior, restart results, or system readiness status
Indicator lights, warnings, or console status messages
Final scanner status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Philips Ingenuity Series CT Scanner - Table Movement or Alignment Issues
description: Troubleshooting guide for Philips Ingenuity Series CT Scanner table movement, positioning, or alignment problems caused by obstruction, table load, controls, accessories, or external setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop use of the CT table if movement is unexpected, uneven, noisy, or inaccurate. Remove the patient from the table if safe to do so. Use proper patient transfer methods. Do not continue scanning if table position is unreliable. Notify CT staff that the scanner is unavailable until checked. Expected outcome: Patient is safely removed from risk and no scan is performed with questionable table positioning.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Table Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what the table is doing before troubleshooting. Ask CT staff what was observed. Check whether the issue involves vertical movement, horizontal travel, table indexing, alignment, docking, or positioning accuracy. Note whether the issue occurred during setup, scan planning, scan acquisition, or patient unloading. Attempt movement only with no patient on the table. Expected outcome: The reported symptom is confirmed without placing a patient at risk.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstructions Around the Table
steps.instructions: External obstruction is one of the first causes to rule out. Inspect around the table base, table rails, gantry opening, foot end, and floor area. Remove linens, straps, patient belongings, step stools, trash cans, IV poles, cables, or positioning aids that may interfere with travel. Verify nothing is caught beneath or alongside the tabletop. Expected outcome: Table movement returns to normal if an obstruction was preventing travel. If this resolves the issue, return the area to safe operating condition and document the obstruction found.

steps.title: Verify Table Load and Patient Positioning
steps.instructions: Excessive load or uneven patient positioning can affect table motion or alignment. Confirm the table was not overloaded. Check whether the patient was centered and positioned properly. Verify that large accessories, immobilizers, or transfer boards were not creating drag or imbalance. Remove unnecessary accessories and retest table motion unloaded. Expected outcome: Table moves smoothly when load or accessory interference is corrected.

steps.title: Inspect Table Pads, Accessories, and Cradles
steps.instructions: Misplaced accessories can create alignment complaints or mechanical resistance. Check that the table pad is seated correctly. Verify head holders, arm boards, cushions, immobilizers, and positioning accessories are properly installed. Look for accessories extending into the gantry path. Confirm nothing is contacting the gantry bore during table travel. Expected outcome: Table movement and alignment improve once accessories are seated or removed.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Interlock Status
steps.instructions: An active emergency stop or safety interlock may inhibit table movement. Inspect emergency stop buttons at the gantry, console, and nearby control areas. Confirm no emergency stop is engaged. Check for visible safety, interlock, or motion inhibit messages on the operator console or gantry display. Reset only according to site policy and manufacturer-approved user procedures. Expected outcome: Table movement is restored if motion was inhibited by an active stop or interlock.

steps.title: Confirm Power and System Status
steps.instructions: Table motion depends on stable system power and proper scanner readiness. Verify the scanner is powered normally. Confirm the operator console is responsive. Check for system warnings, table errors, or gantry communication messages. Confirm the scanner is not in a service, shutdown, paused, or faulted state. Expected outcome: Table commands respond normally when the system is fully ready.

steps.title: Test Table Movement From Available Controls
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is isolated to one control location. With the table unloaded, test movement from the gantry controls. Test movement from the operator console if available. Check whether one direction, speed, or control source fails while others work. Do not force repeated motion if abnormal noise, jerking, or binding occurs. Expected outcome: Isolating the failure helps determine whether the issue is related to controls, communication, or the table drive system.

steps.title: Check for Table Alignment or Indexing Errors
steps.instructions: Alignment issues may appear as inaccurate scan location, incorrect table position, or mismatch between planned and actual anatomy. Ask CT staff whether the laser alignment, scout image, or scan start location appeared incorrect. Verify the patient was positioned correctly using external landmarks. Check whether the table position display changes consistently during movement. Review whether the issue occurred after patient repositioning, accessory changes, or system restart. Expected outcome: Misalignment caused by setup or positioning is corrected before assuming a scanner fault.

steps.title: Restart the Scanner Only If Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: A software or communication fault may affect table commands. Confirm no patient is on the table. Follow site-approved shutdown and restart procedures. Allow the system to fully reboot. Retest unloaded table movement and positioning response. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication faults may clear after restart. If the issue clears, perform basic operational checks before returning the scanner to use.

steps.title: Listen for Abnormal Mechanical Symptoms
steps.instructions: Unusual table behavior may indicate a drive, encoder, brake, sensor, or mechanical alignment problem. During unloaded movement, listen for grinding, slipping, knocking, clicking, or motor strain. Observe for jerky travel, drift, uneven elevation, delayed response, or failure to stop accurately. Check for unusual heat or smell near the table base. Stop testing immediately if movement appears unsafe. Expected outcome: Abnormal mechanical signs indicate the table should not remain in clinical use.

steps.title: Do Not Force Table Motion
steps.instructions: Forcing movement can worsen the fault or create a patient safety hazard. Do not push, pull, override, or repeatedly command the table if it is binding or misaligning. Do not attempt internal table disassembly during first-line troubleshooting. Preserve the error condition for service evaluation when possible. Expected outcome: The scanner is protected from further damage and the fault can be properly evaluated.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported that the Philips Ingenuity CT table was not moving smoothly and appeared misaligned during patient positioning.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no floor obstruction or accessory interference, and the table continued to show inconsistent horizontal positioning during unloaded movement testing.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to CT service for table drive and position feedback evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, table error messages, alignment warnings, or position error messages recorded.
Accessories swapped, removed, or inspected, including table pads, head holders, arm boards, cushions, immobilizers, and transfer boards.
Power behavior, outlet and system power status, console responsiveness, and scanner readiness.
Environmental factors, including obstructions around the table, gantry, rails, floor area, cables, IV poles, step stools, linens, or trash cans.
Indicator lights, emergency stop status, interlock messages, gantry display warnings, and motion inhibit messages.
Final device status listed as returned to service or out of service.

title: Philips Ingenuity Series CT Scanner - Cooling System or Temperature Alarms
