description: Cooling or temperature alarms caused by room heat, blocked airflow, chiller issues, water flow problems, or system thermal limits.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: If the CT scanner is alarming during patient setup or scanning, stop use until the cause is understood. Action: Move the patient off the table if scanning cannot continue safely. Cancel or delay the exam if tube cooling, gantry cooling, or system temperature alarms are active. Notify CT staff that the scanner should not be used until cleared. Expected outcome: The patient is safely removed from a scanner that may be unable to complete imaging reliably. Why it matters: Cooling and temperature faults can lead to scan interruption, exposure inhibit, tube protection shutdowns, image quality issues, or equipment damage. If this resolves an immediate patient safety concern, stop clinical use and continue troubleshooting only when safe.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Alarm or Temperature Message
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm, warning, or system message. Action: Ask CT staff what message appeared. Check whether the alarm occurred during startup, idle, tube warm-up, scanning, or after repeated high-load exams. Record any displayed temperature, cooling, chiller, gantry, or tube heat message. Note whether scanning is inhibited or only delayed. Expected outcome: The issue is clearly identified as a cooling, temperature, or thermal protection problem. Why it matters: A tube heat warning, room temperature warning, chiller fault, and internal gantry temperature alarm may all require different responses.

steps.title: 3. Check the CT Room Temperature
steps.instructions: Verify that the scan room is within the site’s required environmental range. Action: Check the room thermostat or independent temperature reading. Ask staff if the room recently became unusually warm. Check whether the HVAC system is operating normally. Look for blocked vents, closed dampers, or temporary airflow restrictions. Expected outcome: The room temperature is stable and appropriate for CT scanner operation. Why it matters: CT scanners generate significant heat. A warm room can cause the system to reach thermal limits even when the scanner itself is functioning normally. If the room is too warm, contact Facilities/HVAC, keep the scanner out of clinical use if alarms persist, and recheck after the room stabilizes.

steps.title: 4. Inspect Airflow Around the Gantry and Equipment Cabinets
steps.instructions: Check for blocked vents or poor ventilation around the scanner. Action: Inspect the gantry, console area, power cabinets, and equipment cabinets. Remove any linens, boxes, cleaning supplies, paperwork, or stored items near vents. Confirm that required clearance around cabinets and vents is not obstructed. Listen for abnormal fan noise if accessible externally. Expected outcome: Air intake and exhaust paths are clear, and airflow is not blocked. Why it matters: Restricted airflow can cause internal temperature alarms, cabinet overheating, or intermittent shutdowns. If clearing the obstruction resolves the alarm and the system passes normal startup checks, return to service per site procedure.

steps.title: 5. Check for Obvious Chiller or Cooling Unit Problems
steps.instructions: If the site uses an external chiller or cooling unit, inspect it externally only. Action: Verify the chiller is powered on. Check for visible alarms or fault indicators on the chiller display. Confirm the emergency stop or disconnect has not been activated. Look for visible leaks, water on the floor, kinked hoses, or disconnected lines. Confirm the chiller area has adequate airflow and is not blocked by storage. Expected outcome: The chiller is powered, not visibly leaking, and not showing an obvious external fault. Why it matters: Loss of chilled water flow, chiller power, or coolant circulation can trigger scanner temperature alarms and prevent scanning. If the chiller has active faults, leaks, or is not running, remove the scanner from service and escalate to Facilities, OEM service, or the appropriate cooling system vendor.

steps.title: 6. Check Cooling Water or Coolant Indicators
steps.instructions: Review any externally visible coolant level, pressure, or flow indicators if available at your site. Action: Look for low-level indicators, pressure gauges, or flow alarms without opening service panels. Do not add coolant unless site procedure and manufacturer guidance allow it. Check whether staff recently reported water leaks, pump noise, or repeated cooling faults. Expected outcome: No obvious low coolant, flow, or pressure issue is seen externally. Why it matters: Low coolant, poor circulation, or reduced flow may allow the scanner to overheat even if the room temperature is normal. If coolant level, pressure, or flow appears abnormal, stop troubleshooting and escalate for service evaluation.

steps.title: 7. Review Scanner Use Pattern Before the Alarm
steps.instructions: Determine whether the scanner was under heavy thermal load. Action: Ask whether the alarm occurred after multiple contrast studies, trauma scans, cardiac studies, long helical scans, or repeated rescans. Check whether the system displayed tube heat or cooling delay messages. Allow the scanner to cool if the system instructs a cooling wait period. Expected outcome: The scanner either cools down normally after a high-load period or the alarm persists beyond normal cooldown. Why it matters: Some temperature warnings are protective delays after heavy tube usage, while persistent alarms may indicate a cooling system fault. If the scanner cools normally and no alarm returns, document the event and return to service per site policy.

steps.title: 8. Perform a Controlled System Restart Only If Safe
steps.instructions: A software or sensor communication fault can sometimes present as a temperature warning. Action: Confirm no patient is on the table. Follow normal shutdown procedures from the operator console. Allow the system to power down completely. Restart the system and observe for repeated cooling or temperature alarms. Expected outcome: The alarm clears if it was a temporary communication or software state issue. Why it matters: A restart can clear transient faults, but it should not be used to bypass a real overheating or coolant problem. If the alarm returns, do not continue restarting repeatedly.

steps.title: 9. Check for Odor, Heat, Smoke, or Unusual Noise
steps.instructions: Look for signs of equipment damage or unsafe operation. Action: Stand near the gantry, cabinets, and chiller area without opening covers. Check for unusual heat, burning smell, coolant odor, smoke, loud fan noise, pump noise, or vibration. Ask staff whether the alarm was accompanied by shutdowns or abnormal sounds. Expected outcome: No unsafe smell, heat, smoke, leak, or abnormal noise is present. Why it matters: These signs may indicate fan failure, pump failure, electrical overheating, coolant leak, or internal component damage. If smoke, burning smell, fluid leak, or abnormal heat is present, remove the scanner from service immediately and escalate.

steps.title: 10. Confirm Scanner Status After Basic Corrections
steps.instructions: After room temperature, airflow, power, and chiller checks are complete, confirm whether the scanner can operate normally. Action: Verify the alarm is cleared. Confirm the system completes startup without temperature warnings. Ask CT staff to perform approved daily checks, warm-up, or air calibration only if safe and allowed by site procedure. Monitor for alarm recurrence during idle and normal preparation. Expected outcome: The scanner remains stable with no cooling or temperature alarms. Why it matters: A one-time reset is not enough. The scanner must remain thermally stable before being returned to clinical use. If the alarm does not return and all checks pass, document the corrective action and return the scanner to service.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported a cooling system temperature alarm on the Philips Ingenuity CT scanner during exam setup, preventing scanning.
CCR.Cause: Room temperature was elevated and airflow around the equipment cabinet was partially blocked by stored supplies.
CCR.Resolution: Removed obstruction, notified Facilities to correct room temperature, allowed system to stabilize, restarted scanner, and verified no temperature alarm returned.

helpfulDetails: Air vents and cabinet clearances checked
Chiller powered on or showing fault
When the alarm occurred during workflow
Unusual heat, odor, vibration, or noise
